Arguments.prompt: Download US state boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and US county boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Each is a ZIP archive containing a shapefile set; download and unzip both archives in memory or to a temporary directory. Load the state shapefile using geopandas and filter to the single row where the NAME field equals 'Oregon'. Load the county shapefile using geopandas and filter to the single row where the NAME field equals 'Benton' and the STATEFP field matches Oregon's STATEFP value (which is '41'). Compute the bounding box of the Benton County geometry by calling .total_bounds on the filtered county GeoDataFrame, yielding (minx, miny, maxx, maxy) in longitude/latitude degrees. Expand this bounding box by 0.5 degrees in each direction: outer_minx = minx - 0.5, outer_miny = miny - 0.5, outer_maxx = maxx + 0.5, outer_maxy = maxy + 0.5. Create a GeoDataFrame representing this outer bounding box as a single rectangular polygon using shapely.geometry.box(outer_minx, outer_miny, outer_maxx, outer_maxy) with CRS EPSG:4326. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt. This file is a fixed-width text file with no header. Each line has the following fields at these character positions: STATION (columns 1-11, 0-indexed 0:11), LATITUDE (columns 13-20, 0-indexed 12:20), LONGITUDE (columns 22-30, 0-indexed 21:30), ELEMENT (columns 32-35, 0-indexed 31:35), FIRSTYEAR (columns 37-40, 0-indexed 36:40), LASTYEAR (columns 42-45, 0-indexed 41:45). Parse each line by splitting on whitespace: field 0 is STATION, field 1 is LATITUDE (float), field 2 is LONGITUDE (float), field 3 is ELEMENT, field 4 is FIRSTYEAR (int), field 5 is LASTYEAR (int). Import the current year using datetime.date.today().year. Keep only rows where ELEMENT is 'TMAX' or 'TMIN' and LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current year. For each station that appears in the filtered inventory, check whether it has both a TMAX record and a TMIN record; keep only stations that have both. Filter those stations to only those whose LATITUDE falls within [outer_miny, outer_maxy] and whose LONGITUDE falls within [outer_minx, outer_maxx]. Create a GeoDataFrame from these stations with point geometries using shapely.geometry.Point(longitude, latitude) and CRS EPSG:4326, retaining columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE. Save the following four files: (1) State.geojson — the Oregon state GeoDataFrame in GeoJSON format; (2) County.geojson — the Benton County GeoDataFrame in GeoJSON format; (3) OuterBoundary.geojson — the expanded bounding box GeoDataFrame in GeoJSON format, including properties outer_minx, outer_miny, outer_maxx, outer_maxy stored as attributes on the feature; (4) Stations.geojson — the filtered stations GeoDataFrame in GeoJSON format with columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry. Upload all four files to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot: WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson.

Download the file WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson from the S3 data store. Load it as a GeoDataFrame using geopandas. It contains columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry (Point geometries in EPSG:4326). Determine the target week as follows: take today's date using datetime.date.today(), subtract 28 days to get a reference date, then find the most recent Monday on or before that reference date by subtracting reference_date.weekday() days (where Monday=0). That Monday is the week_start. The week_end is week_start plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in the GeoDataFrame, construct the URL https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the value in the STATION column. Download the CSV file for each station using an HTTP GET request. The CSV has a header row; relevant columns are DATE (format YYYY-MM-DD), TMIN (integer or float, in tenths of a degree Celsius), and TMAX (integer or float, in tenths of a degree Celsius). Parse the DATE column as dates. Filter rows to only those where DATE falls within [week_start, week_end] inclusive. Drop any rows where TMIN or TMAX is missing (NaN or empty). If after filtering a station has zero valid rows, skip that station entirely and do not include it in the output. For stations with at least one valid row, compute the mean of TMIN across all remaining rows and the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows. Divide both means by 10.0 to convert from tenths-of-a-degree Celsius to whole degrees Celsius. Collect results into a list of records with fields: STATION (string), TMIN (float, Celsius), TMAX (float, Celsius), and geometry (the Point geometry from the original Stations.geojson for that station). Build a GeoDataFrame from these records with CRS EPSG:4326 and columns STATION, TMIN, TMAX, geometry. Save this GeoDataFrame as TemperatureData.geojson in GeoJSON format. Also save a plain text file called TargetWeek.txt containing exactly two lines: the first line is week_start in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) and the second line is week_end in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). Upload both files to the S3 data store: WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson and WeatherGeographicPlot/TargetWeek.txt.

Download the following files from the S3 data store: WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, and WeatherGeographicPlot/TargetWeek.txt. Load OuterBoundary.geojson as a GeoDataFrame; extract the bounding box of its geometry using .total_bounds to get (outer_minx, outer_miny, outer_maxx, outer_maxy). Load TemperatureData.geojson as a GeoDataFrame with columns STATION, TMIN, TMAX, and geometry (Point geometries in EPSG:4326). Load State.geojson and County.geojson as GeoDataFrames. Read TargetWeek.txt; the first line is week_start (YYYY-MM-DD) and the second line is week_end (YYYY-MM-DD). Parse week_start as a datetime.date object. Format the figure supertitle as: 'Temperature Heatmap for Benton County for week starting <weekday_name>, <month_name> <day>, <year>' where weekday_name is the full English weekday name of week_start (e.g. Monday), month_name is the full English month name, day is the zero-padded day, and year is the four-digit year. Extract station longitudes as an array from the x coordinates of the geometry column, station latitudes from the y coordinates, TMIN values as a float array, and TMAX values as a float array. Build a 100x100 interpolation grid: grid_lon = numpy.linspace(outer_minx, outer_maxx, 100) and grid_lat = numpy.linspace(outer_miny, outer_maxy, 100), then grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d = numpy.meshgrid(grid_lon, grid_lat). Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values onto the grid: grid_tmin = scipy.interpolate.griddata((station_lons, station_lats), tmin_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Similarly interpolate TMAX: grid_tmax = scipy.interpolate.griddata((station_lons, station_lats), tmax_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(18, 8)). For the left subplot (ax1, TMIN): call ax1.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmin, levels=20, cmap='Blues_r', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax1 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Overlay station scatter points on ax1 using ax1.scatter(station_lons, station_lats, c=tmin_values, cmap='Blues_r', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, s=60). Plot the State GeoDataFrame boundary on ax1 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='black', linewidth=1.0). Plot the County GeoDataFrame boundary on ax1 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='darkred', linewidth=1.5). Set ax1.set_xlim(outer_minx, outer_maxx) and ax1.set_ylim(outer_miny, outer_maxy). Set ax1.set_aspect((outer_maxx - outer_minx) / (outer_maxy - outer_miny)). Set x ticks at every 0.5 degrees using numpy.arange(round(outer_minx, 1), outer_maxx + 0.5, 0.5) and y ticks similarly. Set ax1.set_xlabel('Longitude') and ax1.set_ylabel('Latitude'). Set ax1.set_title('Minimum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'). For the right subplot (ax2, TMAX): call ax2.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmax, levels=20, cmap='Reds', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax2 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Overlay station scatter points on ax2 using ax2.scatter(station_lons, station_lats, c=tmax_values, cmap='Reds', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, s=60). Plot the State GeoDataFrame boundary on ax2 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='black', linewidth=1.0). Plot the County GeoDataFrame boundary on ax2 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='darkred', linewidth=1.5). Set ax2.set_xlim(outer_minx, outer_maxx) and ax2.set_ylim(outer_miny, outer_maxy). Set ax2.set_aspect((outer_maxx - outer_minx) / (outer_maxy - outer_miny)). Set x ticks at every 0.5 degrees and y ticks similarly. Set ax2.set_xlabel('Longitude') and ax2.set_ylabel('Latitude'). Set ax2.set_title('Maximum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'). Call fig.suptitle with the formatted supertitle string described above, fontsize=14. Call plt.tight_layout(). Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png using plt.savefig('TemperatureHeatmap.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight'). Upload TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store as WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureHeatmap.png.
